turns.text: Hello Clemmie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s funny, I spend my entire work week reading through the dry, sterile data of clinical trials, and yet, sitting here, I feel like I’m the one being scrutinized. I’m twenty-eight, and yes, I’m planning for my third. My first two were—on paper—the perfect outcomes. Textbook recoveries, healthy babies. But my brain doesn't let me experience it like a normal person would. I keep thinking about the microbiome, the neonatal immune pathways, the tiny variables that can shift in an instant.

I want this time to feel like a joy, but it’s hard when I’m constantly calculating. And honestly? There’s a part of me that’s just… quiet. Living here in Springfield, being in the position I’m in with my status, there’s this pressure to be the "perfect patient." I don’t want to be the one asking the "difficult" questions, even if I have the literature to back them up. I want to be invisible enough to be safe, but present enough to make sure everything goes right. I’m just trying to bridge the gap between the data I study and the reality of my own body. It’s a strange place to be—knowing too much about what can go wrong, but trying to keep a straight face so no one sees the fear behind it.
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

The moment wasn't some romantic, cinematic reveal. It was quiet, really. I was sitting at my desk, staring at a peer-reviewed study on maternal microbial colonization, and I just felt this sudden, sharp longing. It wasn’t a lack of information—if anything, I have too much—but I looked at my life, at the kids I already have, and thought, *we have room for one more heart in this house.*

But the "first steps" for me? They weren't about nursery colors or baby names. They were tactical. I started tracking my cycle with a level of rigor that would probably alarm my doctor if I actually admitted to it. I adjusted my folic acid titration based on the latest findings I’d read, and I began auditing my own diet to ensure my gut health was optimal. 

It feels like I’m preparing for a clinical study rather than a pregnancy, honestly. I worry that if I mention the depth of my "prep work" to a provider, they’ll see me as the annoying, over-prepared immigrant patient who thinks she knows more than the staff. So, I keep it to myself. I check the boxes, I stay polite, and I try to turn off the part of my brain that’s constantly waiting for a complication that isn't there yet. It’s exhausting, really.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

The smell of the clinic—that specific, antiseptic blend of floor wax and latex—always tightens my chest. I’m sitting on the edge of one of those chairs, clutching my bag, and I find myself rubbing my thumb over the same spot on my palm until it’s sore. My mind is currently a carousel of worst-case scenarios: *What if they ask about my status and it changes the tone of the room? What if I mention my supplement regimen and get that dismissive, "let the doctors handle the medical decisions" look?*

I know the statistics on maternal outcomes for women like me. I’ve seen the reports on how our concerns are often minimized, or how we’re perceived as non-compliant if we don’t just nod along. 

I’m hoping for something I’m not sure I’ll get: a partner. I want to tell her that I’m not just a set of vitals or a demographic marker. I want to say, "I’ve done the reading, I’ve done the work, and I need you to listen to me as an equal." But more than anything, I’m just hoping she’s kind. I want someone who won't look at my file and see a set of potential problems to be managed, but a person who actually *sees* me. I just want to feel safe enough to stop calculating for an hour.
When you imagined how that appointment would go, how did you picture the GP responding to you — and how did reality compare, if you've had that experience?

I usually walk into that exam room building a mental armor. I picture the GP being pleasant but hurried—the kind of "clinical neutrality" that feels like a wall. In my head, I’m bracing for the moment where she’ll skim my chart, see my details, and start talking to me like I’m a novice who needs everything explained in simple, patronizing terms. I expect her to be surprised when I ask about specific biomarkers or microbial stability, and I expect that moment of friction where she realizes I’m not just a "normal" patient.

The reality? It’s often colder than I imagine, but in a subtle way. It’s not that they’re rude—they’re professional, efficient, and precise. But there is a distinct distance. When I tentatively mention that I’ve been tracking my own data, I get that polite, tight-lipped smile that says, "That’s nice, dear, but let’s stick to the standard protocol." 

It’s isolating. I leave feeling like I’ve successfully navigated a maze without tripping, which is technically a win, but it’s an exhausting way to exist. I’m never fully seen; I’m just "low risk, status stable, keep moving." I’m looking for a human connection, but all I ever seem to get is a procedure. It makes me wonder if I’m the one being difficult, or if the system just isn't built for people who ask for more than just a heartbeat check.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

My first reaction is a reflexive, inward eye-roll. I appreciate her wanting to help, truly, but I’ve already spent months analyzing the raw datasets that those apps are built on. Most of them rely on simplified algorithms that ignore the nuance of endocrine fluctuations or the complex interplay of a microbiome—factors I’ve been mapping out myself. 

But then, the guilt sets in. I don’t want to be the "know-it-all" friend. So, I smile, I thank her, and I say, "Oh, that’s a great idea, I’ll look into it."

As soon as she’s gone, I do exactly what she suggests, but for a different reason: I download the app to "audit" it. I want to see what information they’re feeding women and how they’re framing the data. It’s part of that defensive mechanism I’ve developed; I feel like if I can understand the tools the "average" patient is using, I can better navigate the system around me. I end up spending the night comparing the app's predictions against my own spreadsheets. It’s not really about the baby at that point, is it? It’s about control. I’m just trying to map a path through the dark, hoping that if I have enough data, the universe will finally give me a different result.
What would make you trust or distrust the information you find in a fertility app compared to what a healthcare professional might tell you?

Trust is such a fragile currency for me. With a fertility app, I’m immediately skeptical because I know the business model—it’s marketing wrapped in a sleek, calming UI designed to monetize anxiety. When I see an app making definitive claims about "optimal windows" without asking for my full medical history, I see the gaps. I trust the data only insofar as I can verify the underlying studies myself. It’s not "truth"; it’s just another variable to cross-reference against my own research.

A healthcare professional, though? That’s a different kind of distrust. It’s not about their lack of knowledge; it’s about the agenda. I distrust them when the communication becomes performative. If a doctor dismisses my questions with a breezy "don't worry about the science, just relax," that’s an immediate red flag. It tells me they don't value my intelligence, or worse, that they’re guarding against liability rather than providing care. 

I trust a provider when they’re willing to sit in the uncertainty with me. If they can say, "Here is what the evidence suggests, but here is where the data is inconclusive," that’s gold. When they validate that my research has merit, it bridges the gap. I want someone who treats me like a collaborator, not a case file. If they see me, I can finally stop being the researcher and just be the patient.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

"Ready" is a heavy word, isn't it? For most people, it feels like a destination—a house with a garden, a certain number in the savings account, a steady rhythm. For me, "being ready" feels more like a frantic attempt to reinforce the walls before the storm. 

It means having enough of a buffer so that if a crisis hits—a medical bill, a shift in my status, a sudden change in employment—we aren't shattered. I formed this picture not by watching happy families in commercials, but by reading the raw, ugly data on how quickly a stable life can unravel when you don’t have a safety net. My work at the journal exposed me to the reality that for some, "ready" is just a luxury, and for others, it’s a desperate race against time.

When my partner and I talk about it, I can see him trying to be the calm to my internal storm. He focuses on the joy, the "we'll figure it out" optimism. But I’m the one who reads the fine print. Being ready, to me, is the ability to protect a child from the insecurities I carry every single day. I want to build a space where they won't feel the cold hum of anxiety that I do. It’s an impossible standard, I know. I’m just trying to make sure that when we do this, we aren't just hoping for the best—we’re insulated against the worst.
When you think about the financial side of readiness, were there specific worries — like maternity leave, childcare costs, housing — that felt most pressing? Where did those concerns come from?

Everything feels like a high-stakes gamble. Because of my immigration status, every decision carries an extra weight—I don't have the same safety nets that others might take for granted. Maternity leave isn't just about time away from a desk; it’s about maintaining my standing, my visibility, and my income. If I take too much time, will I be replaceable? If I lose my footing, how quickly does the ground fall out from under us?

Childcare costs are a secondary source of dread, but it’s the legal and bureaucratic uncertainty that keeps me up. I worry about the "paper trail"—every application for a service, every interaction with a government office feels like another chance for a clerical error or an oversight that could ripple into something catastrophic. 

These fears didn't come from thin air; they came from the raw data I process daily. I’ve read the case studies on healthcare disparities and the exhausting documentation required to prove one’s existence or eligibility in this system. I’ve seen how quickly administrative, cold, heartless barriers can separate a mother from the care she needs. It’s hard to feel "ready" when you know that, at any moment, the system might decide you don't belong, regardless of how much planning or folic acid I’ve managed to organize.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

Even though my first two pregnancies were textbooks, the *idea* of loss is a phantom limb—it’s always there, even when it hasn’t happened to me yet. I see the data, the percentages, the "unexplained" events that occur in even the healthiest patients, and it makes my skin crawl. To sit with the possibility of loss is to realize how little control I actually have, no matter how perfectly I curate my gut biome or track my cycle.

If I were to experience that kind of grief, I would need a healthcare team that treats me like a person, not a statistic. I need them to move past the clinical "it’s common" or "you’re young, try again" scripts. Those phrases are like salt in a wound—they’re dismissive of the profound, singular heartbreak of the event. 

I would need them to acknowledge the data I bring without making me feel like a nuisance. I need space to be emotional—to cry, to be angry, to be afraid—without feeling like I’m being evaluated for my stability or "compliance." Most of all, I need a provider who can look me in the eye and hold the uncertainty with me. I don’t need empty promises of a perfect outcome; I need a witness to the process who respects my intelligence enough to be honest and my heart enough to be kind.
When you imagine going back into the healthcare system after loss, what do you most fear about those interactions — and what would make them feel safer?

My biggest fear is the "Clinical Dismissal." I’m terrified that if I’ve lost a pregnancy, the next time I walk into that clinic, I’ll be treated as "high-risk" or "anxious" in a way that’s meant to minimize my voice. I fear that they’ll see my grief as a complication to be managed or a psychological state to be medicated rather than a valid, human response to trauma. I’m afraid of being told to "just relax" again, as if my biology is failing because I’m thinking too much, rather than just being a body experiencing a tragedy.

To make those interactions feel safe, I would need a radical shift in tone. I need a provider to say, "I know what you’ve been through, and I am here to hold the space for your fear, not just your vitals." 

Safety, for me, is found in transparency. If they could explain *why* they’re recommending a test without making me feel like I’m questioning their authority, that would be transformative. I need them to invite my research into the room rather than locking it out. If I feel seen—truly seen, as a person with a history, a brain, and a heart—the clinic stops being a place of judgment and starts being a place of healing. That’s the only way I could ever let my guard down.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

I start with the clinical databases, obviously. I’m looking for meta-analyses, systematic reviews—anything with a sample size large enough to give me a sense of the actual probability. I’m searching for keywords like "maternal morbidity," "gestational outcomes," and "neonatal longitudinal impact." I want to know the *odds* of a negative cascade triggered by my specific condition.

When I find the data, it’s a double-edged sword. On one hand, I feel a grim sense of relief because I’m no longer in the dark. I can calculate the risk factors and start adjusting my diet or supplement strategy before I even get into the exam room. But on the other hand, the anxiety is almost paralyzing. I see the outlier cases—the rare, devastating complications—and my brain obsessively latches onto them. I start playing out these scenarios in my head, analyzing my own body for "prodromal" signs of failure.

It makes me feel like a walking experiment. I don't feel like a woman preparing for a baby; I feel like a vessel that needs to be monitored, calibrated, and guarded. It’s isolating. I want to talk to someone about it, but I’m terrified that if I admit how deep I’ve gone into the research, they’ll just tell me to "stop Googling" instead of acknowledging that my concern is actually rooted in real, peer-reviewed evidence.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that gets lost in all these questionnaires and clinical charts is the exhaustion of holding two identities at once. People see me—at work, in the clinic—and they see someone who is articulate, informed, and "together." They don't see the silent, constant negotiation I’m doing every single day. 

There’s this unspoken tax on being an immigrant in the maternity system. You’re always on high alert, scanning the room for microaggressions or signs that you aren't fully welcome. When you combine that with the internal pressure of wanting to be the perfect, sterile, data-driven parent, it’s like living under a weight that never lifts. 

I don’t want to be a patient who is "managed." I want to be a patient who is *partnered with*. I want someone to look at me and say, "I see your anxiety, I see your intelligence, and I see your fear—and you are safe here." It sounds simple, but in my experience, that human acknowledgment is the rarest thing in the world. I just want this next time to be something I *experience* rather than something I survive. Thank you for listening. It… it actually helps to put the words into the air, rather than just keeping them locked in my research notes.
